title: The Squatter, Three Cornstalks, And The Well
content:
(A Dirge of Sin and Sorrow, Sung by Joe Swallow)

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ There was a Squatter in the land,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So runs the truthful tale I tell,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ There also were three cornstalks, and
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ There also was the Squatterן¿½s Well.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Singing (slowly): ן¿½Sin and sorrer, sin and sor-rer, sin and sor-r-r-rer.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The Squatter he was full of pluck,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The Cornstalks they were full of sin,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The well it was half full of muck
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That many rains had drifted in.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Singing (with increased feeling): ן¿½Sin, &c.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The Squatter hired the Cornstalks Three
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To cleanse the well of mud and clay;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And so they started willing-lee
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ At five-and-twenty bob a day.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Singing (apprehensively): ן¿½Sin, &c.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ At five-and-twenty bob the lot,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Thatן¿½s eight-and-four the day would bring
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To each; and so they thought theyן¿½d got
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A rather soft and easy thing.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Singing (sadly): ן¿½Sin, &c.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The Cornstalks cleaned the well within
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A day or two, or thereabout,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And then they worked an awful sin,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A scheme to make the job last out.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Singing (reproachfully): ן¿½Sin and sorrer, &c.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For when the well was cleaned out quite
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of all its logs and muck and clay
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ They tipped a drayload down at night
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And worked to haul it up next day.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Singing (dismally): ן¿½Sin, &c.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But first the eldest, christened Hodge,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He greased the dray-wheel axles, so
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The super wouldnן¿½t smell the dodge
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And couldnן¿½t let the Squatter know.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Singing (hopelessly): ן¿½Sin and sorrer, &c.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The stuff they surfaced out each day
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With some surprise the Squatter saw.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He never dreamt the sand and clay
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Was three miles off the night before.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Singing (mournfully): ן¿½Sin and sorrer, &c.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But he got something in his eye;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ It wasnן¿½t green, thatן¿½s very plain.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He said the well was rather dry,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And they could fill it up again.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Singing (mournfully and dismally): ן¿½Sin and sorrer, &c.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The Cornstalks went to work next day
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In hope, of course, of extra tin,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The Squatter watched, and, sad to say,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The mullock wouldnן¿½t all go in.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Singing (with great pathos): ן¿½Sin and sorrer, &c.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And though the Cornstalks twigged the ruse
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Whereby the boss had done ן¿½em brown,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ They argued that the clay was loose,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And wanted time to settle down.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Singing (hopelessly): ן¿½Sin and sorrer, &c.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The boss began to rave and tear,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And yelled with a most awful frown,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½I will not settle up, I swear,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Till that there clay is settled down!ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Singing (hopefully): ן¿½Sin, &c.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Before my cheques yerן¿½Il pocket, boys,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Yerן¿½ll put a mountain in a wellן¿½,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The Cornstalks didnן¿½t make a noise,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ They only murmured sadly, !

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Singing (triumphantly): ן¿½Sin and sorrer, &c.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ MORAL:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ There is a moral to my rhyme,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A moral to the dirge I sing,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That when you do go in for crime
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ You mustnן¿½t overdoo the thing.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Singing (more dismally than ever): ן¿½Sin and sorrer, s-i-n and sor-r-r-r-rer!ן¿½
meaning:
Exploring the themes of classic, Henry Lawson delivers a powerful performance in "The Squatter, Three Cornstalks, And The Well"...

### Why We Love This Line
At Linespedia, we believe that poetry is the ultimate sanctuary for the soul...
